meta.title: First Consultation (FSC) — childhood epilepsy after 1 or more paroxysmal events
meta.authors: van Diessen E, Lamberink HJ, Otte WM, Doornebal N, Brouwer OF, Jansen FE, Braun KPJ
meta.outcome: Probability that a child presenting after one or more paroxysmal events will be diagnosed with epilepsy, from 11 clinical features and the interictal EEG available at the first consultation
meta.cff_info:
What this shows. The First Consultation (FSC) model (van Diessen et al., Pediatrics 2018) estimates the probability that a child who presents after one or more paroxysmal events will be diagnosed with epilepsy, using only information available at the first outpatient consultation: 11 clinical features plus the interictal EEG. Developed at the UMC Utrecht paediatric-neurology outpatient department and validated on a similar cohort from the Martini Hospital, Groningen.

How to read it

  • The result is the percent probability of an epilepsy diagnosis.
  • The EEG is by far the strongest single driver (generalised epileptiform → large increase), followed by automatisms and a history of neurological disorder.
  • Intended to support — not replace — the clinical judgement at that first visit.

Validation. Developed in 451 children referred for diagnostic work-up after one or more paroxysmal events and externally validated in a second cohort (n=187). Discrimination was excellent (AUC 0.86, 95% CI 0.80–0.92; PPV 0.93, NPV 0.76); in the subgroup still diagnostically uncertain after initial work-up, AUC was 0.73.

Source & acknowledgement. This calculator is a faithful re-implementation of the First Consultation (FSC) tool from epilepsypredictiontools.info, developed at UMC Utrecht (Braun / Otte group). The model coefficients were ported verbatim from the authors' own open-source implementation (github.com/wmotte/epilepsypredictiontools, Apache-2.0), reproduced here with the authors' permission. Please cite the original paper and visit epilepsypredictiontools.info for the original tool.

predictors.name: Sex
predictors.assess: Patient sex. Male sex slightly increases the estimated probability of an epilepsy diagnosis (female = reference).

predictors.name: Age at first event
predictors.assess: Age at the first paroxysmal event, in years (model range 0–18; values are clamped to this range). Older age at onset slightly increases the probability.

predictors.name: Bilateral shaking or jerking
predictors.assess: Event description: was there bilateral shaking or jerking?

predictors.name: Stiffening / tonic posturing
predictors.assess: Event description: was there stiffening or tonic posturing?

predictors.name: Lateralisation of symptoms
predictors.assess: Event description: were the symptoms lateralised (clearly one-sided)?

predictors.name: Weakness / loss of muscle tone
predictors.assess: Event description: was there weakness or loss of muscle tone?

predictors.name: Automatisms
predictors.assess: Event description: were there automatisms? One of the strongest clinical predictors in the model.

predictors.name: History of neurological disorder
predictors.assess: History of a neurological disorder — i.e. birth asphyxia, congenital or acquired brain lesion, head trauma, CNS infection, or migraine.

predictors.name: History of psychiatric disorder
predictors.assess: History of a psychiatric disorder — i.e. autism, attention-deficit (hyperactivity) disorder, or other.

predictors.name: Known syndrome
predictors.assess: A known genetic or metabolic syndrome.

predictors.name: EEG result
predictors.assess: Interictal EEG at first consultation. The dominant predictor: generalised or localised epileptiform activity raises the probability of an epilepsy diagnosis substantially; 'hypofunctional' = focal/diffuse slowing without epileptiform discharges.

model.note: Population-level logistic regression from children seen after one or more paroxysmal events. Complements, does not replace, clinical judgement. Original tool: epilepsypredictiontools.info (UMC Utrecht).
model.viz_info: Each clinical feature and the EEG add a weight (the published logistic-regression coefficient) to a linear predictor, which is turned into the probability of an epilepsy diagnosis. The waterfall starts from a baseline child (all features absent, normal EEG) and shows how each finding raises (red) or lowers (green) the probability.

provenance.source: van Diessen et al., Pediatrics 2018;142(6):e20180931 (doi 10.1542/peds.2018-0931, PMID 30389715). Coefficients taken verbatim from the authors' reference implementation at github.com/wmotte/epilepsypredictiontools (docs/js/fsc-calc.js, Apache-2.0), as published at epilepsypredictiontools.info/first-consultation.
provenance.notes: Logistic: logodds = -3.79076 + 0.65583*male + 0.04660*age(0-18, clamped) + 0.58519*bilateral + 0.65431*tonic + 0.76494*lateralisation + 1.12028*loss-of-tone + 1.62695*automatisms + 1.33676*neuro-history + 0.21355*psych-history + 0.79069*syndrome + EEG(normal 0 / generalised 3.80806 / localised 3.20263 / hypofunctional 1.27371); p=100*odds/(1+odds). Coefficients copied exactly from the authors' fsc-calc.js. Added per partner request (Braun/Galovic, Jul 2026) WITH acknowledgement + link to epilepsypredictiontools.info. Built 2026-07-08.
